data.title: Limoneira outlines $10M cost savings and targets 4M–4.5M fresh lemon cartons for FY26 amid business model transformation
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Limoneira Company (LMNR) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Harold Edwards, President and CEO, stated that Limoneira has "systematically addressed the fundamental oversupply in the global lemon market by repositioning our company around multiple profit centers and dramatically improving our cost structure." He emphasized that the return to Sunkist is expected to generate $10 million in cost savings in fiscal year 2026, providing enhanced customer access and strengthening both packing margins and grower partner relationships.
  • Edwards highlighted expansion in avocado offerings, noting 1,500 acres planted, with 700 nonbearing acres set to mature over the next 3 to 4 years, effectively doubling avocado production capacity. He introduced the planned 50-50 organic recycling joint venture with Agromin, expected to process 300,000 tons of organic waste annually and generate $4 million to $5 million in additional EBITDA beginning in fiscal year 2027.
  • Real estate development remains a key pillar, with the Harvest at Limoneira project expected to deliver $155 million in distributions over the next 5 years. Edwards also discussed progress on monetizing water rights, citing $1.7 million realized from Santa Paula Basin water rights sales and a projected $50 million to $70 million from Class 3 Colorado River water rights and Santa Paula Basin rights through 2027.
  • Edwards summarized the transformation: "We've transformed from a commodity lemon producer to a diversified agricultural and real estate company with multiple growth engines and a dramatically improved cost structure."
  • Mark Palamountain, Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer, stated: "For the fourth quarter of fiscal year 2025, total net revenue was $42.8 million compared to total net revenue of $43.9 million in the fourth quarter of the previous fiscal year." He detailed the seasonal business cadence and the expected changes as a result of the Sunkist partnership.

Outlook

  • Edwards projected $10 million in cost savings for fiscal 2026, with benefits from the Sunkist partnership "visible in our fiscal year 2026 results through improved cost structure and enhanced customer relationships."
  • Fresh lemon volumes for fiscal 2026 are guided at 4 million to 4.5 million cartons, and avocado volumes at 5 million to 6 million pounds.
  • The company maintains expectations of $155 million in real estate distributions over the next 5 years and a $4 million to $5 million EBITDA contribution from the Agromin joint venture beginning in fiscal year 2027.

Financial Results

  • Net revenue for Q4 2025 was $42.8 million, with agribusiness revenue at $41.3 million. Fresh packed lemon sales contributed $19.2 million, with 821,000 cartons sold at an average $23.33 per carton.
  • Avocado revenue for the quarter was $300,000, with 396,000 pounds sold at $0.79 per pound. Orange revenue reached $2.9 million, with 148,000 cartons sold at $19.67 per carton.
  • Operating loss for the quarter was $11.1 million, and net loss applicable to common stock was $8.8 million. Net loss per diluted share was $0.49.
  • The results reflect $6.7 million in strategic transformation costs and additional expenses related to storage facility power outages, expected to be recovered through insurance.
  • For fiscal year 2025, total net revenue was $159.7 million, operating loss was $20.4 million, and net loss applicable to common stock was $16.5 million.
  • Long-term debt as of October 31, 2025, was $72.5 million, with $41.6 million of credit facility availability.

Q&A

  • Pooran Sharma, Stephens Inc.: Asked for more details on the $10 million cost savings from the Sunkist partnership and operational efficiencies, as well as the shift in customer relationships. Mark Palamountain explained, "we really took a deeper check at storage first and foremost and all of our operational processes there... renegotiated some of our storage contracts and also utilized some of the Sunkist unused capacities... another $5 million relative to storage and operational efficiencies." Harold Edwards added that returning to Sunkist lowered sales and marketing costs from $1.50 per carton to $0.60 per carton, and provided greater access to top retail customers and better pricing.
  • Sharma also queried about monetizing water assets. Edwards described the value of Santa Paula Basin rights and Class 3 Colorado River rights, explaining that "those water rights are very, very valuable, and most likely will be contributed to following programs, of which will be paid to not use that water."
  • Gerard Sweeney, ROTH Capital Partners: Focused on the Colorado River Compact negotiations and Class 3 water rights. Palamountain confirmed higher values are being discussed for these rights and Edwards noted that cuts are expected for more junior water rights classes, not Class 3.
  • Sweeney also inquired about the Limco Del Mar property. Edwards detailed the complex entitlement and community engagement process required for development and stated that winning an entitlement vote could increase land value significantly.
  • Sweeney asked about avocado production cadence and per-acre profitability. Palamountain outlined planting schedules and projected average operating profit of $12,000 to $14,000 per acre.
  • Mark Smith, Lake Street Capital Markets: Asked for an update on expense savings and balance sheet comfort. Palamountain described ongoing operational streamlining, finalized brokerage business sale, and renegotiated leases, stating, "It will be as we see it and prove it out." He also explained that the company restructured its debt covenants for increased flexibility.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed on cost savings granularity, water rights monetization, real estate development hurdles, and balance sheet management, signaling a slightly skeptical but constructive tone and seeking confirmation of strategic progress.
  • Management adopted a confident and detailed tone in prepared remarks, emphasizing transformation and cost discipline. In the Q&A, management remained thorough and constructive, offering specifics on cost structures, partnership benefits, and long-term asset values.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's confidence increased, shifting from addressing operational challenges to focusing on the execution of strategic initiatives with tangible results, while analysts maintained a pragmatic approach, probing for execution details and risk management.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for fresh lemon volumes shifted from a 4.5–5 million carton range to 4–4.5 million cartons for fiscal 2026. Avocado volume guidance for the coming year is 5–6 million pounds, following a previous year of 7 million pounds due to alternate bearing.
  • The Sunkist partnership's anticipated cost savings increased from $5 million to $10 million, with additional operational efficiencies identified in the current quarter.
  • Management's tone moved from optimism about upcoming strategic execution to confidence in measurable transformation and cost improvements. Analysts continued to focus on execution risks and details, but engaged more deeply on asset monetization and capital allocation.
  • Key metrics such as revenue, losses, and debt levels were explicitly discussed, with current quarter results reflecting higher transformation charges and ongoing strategic asset divestitures.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited volatile commodity pricing, particularly for lemons, as an ongoing risk and emphasized efforts to diversify and reduce exposure.
  • Water rights monetization is subject to regulatory developments, scarcity, and local government negotiations, with management actively engaging in industry discussions and community outreach.
  • Real estate development projects require extensive entitlement and community approval processes, presenting execution risks and potential for delays.
  • Operational transition to Sunkist and ongoing cost restructuring present short-term execution risks, with management monitoring results and adjusting as needed.
  • Analysts focused on the sustainability of cost savings, timing and values of asset divestitures, debt management, and successful execution of new business initiatives.

Final Takeaway

Limoneira management highlighted that fiscal year 2026 will be the launchpad for a transformed business model, marked by a $10 million reduction in SG&A, enhanced customer access through the Sunkist partnership, and a diversified portfolio including increased avocado production and strategic real estate and water asset monetization. With multiple value drivers converging, the company expects these changes to deliver improved financial performance and position Limoneira as a resilient, multi-engine growth company less reliant on commodity lemon pricing.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Good Times Restaurants expects 1.7% average menu price increase at Bad Daddy's Q1 2026 while targeting value promotions
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Good Times Restaurants Inc. (GTIM) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Ryan Zink, President and CEO, described the fourth quarter as “a challenging 1 for us, in particular, at our Good Times concept,” citing “soft sales and higher costs most specifically, the significantly elevated cost of ground beef” impacting profitability. Zink noted a sequential improvement in same-store sales at Good Times, with a 6.6% decline representing a 240 basis point improvement from the prior quarter. For the first 11 weeks of Q1, same-store sales were down approximately 3.6% year-over-year. He highlighted operational changes, including realigned general manager schedules and improved restaurant-level training, aimed at rolling out “true cook to order among all of our burger products.” Zink stated, “We have several different price tiers within our system and remain sensitive to menu price increases as the quick service burger segment has earned a poor reputation recently for value.” Upcoming targeted value promotions and expanded GT Rewards offerings are planned for the spring, with a refreshed mobile app to enhance the ordering experience. For Bad Daddy’s, same-store sales weakened during the quarter but improved sequentially in Q1, down approximately 1.6% for the first 11 weeks. Product innovation included a successful Bavarian pretzel and a new chocolate cookie cheesecake, both being considered for core menu addition, with further menu simplification and a “burger of the month platform” planned. Zink reported “blended year-over-year price increase covering food and beverage of less than 1%” with an expected “average year-over-year price increase for the first quarter of approximately 1.7%.”
  • Keri August, Senior VP of Finance & Accounting, stated, “Total revenues decreased approximately 5.1% for the quarter to $34 million and decreased approximately 0.5% compared to our all-time record fiscal year 2024 sales to $141.6 million.” August detailed that Bad Daddy’s total restaurant sales decreased to $24 million for the quarter and same-store sales fell 4.6%, while Good Times’ sales declined to $9.7 million with same-store sales down 6.6%. August also noted, “Our net loss to common shareholders for the quarter was $3,000 or $0.00 per share versus net income of $0.2 million, $0.02 per share in the fourth quarter last year.”

Outlook

  • Zink projected that “with our upcoming cook-to-order process and continued improvements in ops execution, we believe we can re-earn a premium to those competitors over time,” while remaining averse to broad discounting. August shared, “We expect an average price increase of approximately 1.7% for the first quarter 2026” at Bad Daddy’s and only “modest price increases” at Good Times, with further adjustments dependent on competitive pricing.
  • No formal EPS or revenue guidance was provided. Management emphasized a focus on operational improvements and targeted promotions rather than significant price increases.

Financial Results

  • August reported that total revenues decreased approximately 5.1% for the quarter to $34 million and by 0.5% for the year to $141.6 million. Bad Daddy’s same-store sales fell 4.6% and Good Times’ same-store sales decreased 6.6%. Restaurant-level operating profit at Bad Daddy’s was $2.4 million for the quarter or 9.9% of sales, compared to $3.4 million or 13.2% last year. Good Times’ restaurant-level operating profit fell by $0.4 million to $0.8 million, representing 8% of sales, a 420 basis point decline. Adjusted EBITDA for the quarter was negative $74,000 compared to $1.3 million in Q4 2024.
  • Food and beverage costs increased at both concepts due to “record high ground beef prices,” with food and packaging costs at Good Times rising to 32.1% and Bad Daddy’s food and beverage costs at 31.6%. Labor costs increased as a percentage of sales at both brands due to sales deleverage and wage rate increases. August stated, “We anticipate 6% to 7% general and administrative costs in fiscal 2026.”

Q&A

  • There were no analyst questions during the call. Zink concluded by noting, “the first quarter of fiscal 2026 is shaping to mark improvement in same-store sales and in adjusted EBITDA.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • No analyst sentiment could be determined as there were no questions or comments from analysts during the Q&A.
  • Management’s tone was candid about challenges, with Zink stating, “it goes without saying that we are disappointed in the results and committed to immediate improvement,” but also optimistic about early trends in Q1 and confident in operational initiatives and product roadmap. Zink expressed pride in team efforts and emphasized future improvement.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s prepared remarks remained focused on acknowledging headwinds while communicating steps being taken to address them. The absence of analyst questions removed any direct challenges or skepticism that may have emerged in Q&A.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The Q4 quarter saw a sharper drop in same-store sales at both concepts compared to Q3, but management highlighted sequential improvement in early Q1. Good Times’ same-store sales improved from a 9% decline in Q3 to a 6.6% decline in Q4, and Bad Daddy’s same-store sales worsened from a 1.4% decline in Q3 to 4.6% in Q4, but showed improvement in early Q1. Restaurant-level operating profit margins narrowed further at both concepts. Operational strategies (cook-to-order rollout, targeted value promotions, loyalty app refresh) were more explicitly discussed in Q4. There was no dialogue with analysts in Q4, in contrast to Q3, when analyst questions focused on CapEx, cash accumulation, and underperformance drivers.

Risks and Concerns

  • Risks discussed included market price volatility in ground beef and other proteins, staffing constraints, supply chain and inflationary pressures, uncertain restaurant development plans, increased competition, and cost increases or ingredient shortages. August referenced, “the impact of staffing constraints at our restaurants, the impact of supply chain constraints and inflation, the uncertain nature of current restaurant development plans and the ability to implement those plans and integrate new restaurants.” Management also cited wage and benefit cost increases, potential further labor cost pressures from minimum wage hikes, and the risk of value perception erosion from price sensitivity within the quick service segment. The company is averse to large-scale discounting, instead planning targeted value promotions and monitoring competitive pricing.

Final Takeaway

Management described Q4 as a difficult period marked by declining same-store sales and margin pressures from elevated input and labor costs. Initiatives such as the rollout of cook-to-order burgers, targeted value promotions, and improvements to loyalty programs and mobile ordering are expected to drive operational improvements. Early results in Q1 indicate sequential improvement in same-store sales and adjusted EBITDA, with management emphasizing a commitment to disciplined pricing, cost controls, and enhancing guest experience to support future growth.

data.title: Organogenesis starts rolling submission for ReNu allograft for knee arthritis
data.description:
• Organogenesis (ORGO) said it has begun a rolling BLA submission for ReNu, its cryopreserved, amniotic suspension allograft developed for the management of symptomatic knee arthritis.
  • ReNu is made of amniotic fluid cells and micronized amniotic membrane, and also contains cellular, growth factor, and extracellular matrix components.
  • The application is supported by results from three randomized, controlled trials.
  • Organogenesis said it anticipates the rolling BLA will be completed in H1 2026.

data.title: UiPath, Versant Media to join S&P MidCap 400 and SmallCap 600
data.description:
• S&P Dow Jones Indices said on Tuesday that UiPath (PATH) will replace Synovus Financial (SNV) in the S&P MidCap 400, effective January 2, 2026, following Synovus’s pending acquisition by Pinnacle Financial Partners  (PNFP).
  • Versant Media Group (VSNT) will replace Brandywine Realty Trust (BDN) in the S&P SmallCap 600, effective January 6, 2026, after its spin-off from Comcast (CMCSA), which will remain in the S&P 500.
  • CMCSA -0.0% after hours to $29.38.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Copper Giant upsizes private placement to $12 mln
data.description:
• Copper Giant Resources (TSXV: CGNT) (OTCQB: LBCMF) on Tuesday said it has increased the size of its previously announced non-brokered private placement to $12 million from $10 million.
  • The company said it plans to issue up to 30 million units at C$0.40 per unit.
  • Each unit will consist of one common share and one-half of one warrant, with each whole warrant exercisable at C$0.60 per share for 36 months from closing.
  • Copper Giant said the offering remains subject to approval by the TSX Venture Exchange and that proceeds will be used for working capital and general corporate purposes.
  • CGNT:CA closed -2.0% at $0.49.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: FCPT acquires Olive Garden property for $2.2 million
data.description:
• Four Corners Property Trust (FCPT) announced on Tuesday the acquisition of an Olive Garden property for $2.2 million.
  • The property is located in a trafficked corridor in Illinois and is corporate-operated under a long term, triple net lease with approximately eight years of term remaining, the company said. 
  • The transaction was priced at a 6.7% cap rate on rent as of the closing date and exclusive of transaction costs.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Pelangio Exploration receives $1,395,933 from warrant exercise
data.description:
• Pelangio Exploration (PX:CA) (OTC Pink: PGXPF) on Tuesday said it has received gross proceeds of $1,395,933.35 from the exercise of of common share purchase warrants.
  • The company said 27,918,667 million warrants were exercised at $0.05 per warrant, resulting in the issuance of an equal number of common shares.
  • Pelangio said the funds will be used to advance its projects and for general working capital and corporate purposes.
  • PGXPF closed +11.76% at $0.19.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: StoneCo is the top payment processing services stock in terms of YTD performance
data.description:
As 2025 draws to a close, Wall Street has observed another strong year of gains across most sectors. 

The financial sector has emerged as the fourth best-performing one YTD, with an advance of +14.73%. Its accompanying State Street Financial Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLF) is +14.69% YTD.

Seeking Alpha took a look at the financials subsector that covers companies providing transaction and payment processing services and identified the top YTD performers. StoneCo (STNE) grabbed the number one spot with a +77.4% gain. 

Here is the list:

  1. StoneCo (STNE), Quant Rating: 3.03 Hold, +77.4% YTD.

data.title: Daktronics acquires intellectual property and equipment assets from X Display Company
data.description:
• Daktronics (DAKT) (NASDAQ: DAKT) said on Tuesday it has acquired intellectual property, equipment assets and technical expertise from X Display Company Technology Ltd to expand its capabilities in MicroLED and MicroIC display technologies.
  • The U.S.-based display maker said the acquisition includes proprietary mass-transfer and MicroLED fabrication technologies for display applications, specialized development equipment, and the hiring of 15 former X Display Company employees with expertise in MicroLED, MicroIC and mass-transfer processes.

  • Daktronics did not disclose the financial terms of the transaction.

  • DAKT -0.15% after hours to $18.88.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Xiao-I Corporation receives Nasdaq deficiency notices
data.description:
• Xiao-I Corporation (AIXI) announced on Tuesday that it has received two written notifications from Nasdaq, indicating that the company is not in compliance with certain continued listing requirements for the Nasdaq Global Market.
  • On December 16, 2025, the company received a notice from Nasdaq stating that the company does not currently satisfy a minimum closing bid price of $1.00 per share.
  • The notice was based on the company's American Depositary Shares having a closing bid price below $1.00 per share for 30 consecutive business days from November 3, 2025, through December 15, 2025. In accordance with Nasdaq Listing Rules, the company has been provided a 180-calendar-day compliance period, expiring on June 16, 2026, to regain compliance. 
  • On December 17, 2025, the company received a second notice from Nasdaq stating that the company does not currently satisfy the minimum market value of publicly held shares requirement of at least $15 million. 
  • Source: Press Release
